channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Stephen Colbert’s Final ‘Late Show’ Marks the End of an Era
item.description: Stephen Colbert’s “Colbert Report” satirized politics. Then his “Late Show” confronted a moment when politics became self-satirizing.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
item.media:credit: CQ Roll Call/Associated Press; Scott Kowalchyk/CBS

item.title: Is Museum Wall Text the Next Political Battleground?
item.description: The Smithsonian has changed or eliminated some interpretive language that typically accompanies exhibited artworks. Critics call this self-censorship.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small and Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: This work by Stephanie J. Woods on display at the Smithsonian’s National Portrait Gallery is part of a competition that recognizes excellence in portraiture.

item.title: A Landmark Show Returns, Looking for Blackness in a Personal Way
item.description: The sixth edition of the Studio Museum in Harlem’s group survey is political but inwardly-focused, operating at a quieter metabolism.
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter
item.media:credit: Guarionex Rodriguez for The New York Times
item.media:description: From “Fade,” at the Studio Museum in Harlem, Antonio Darden’s mural-size photograph, “Untitled (Reclining Figure),” 2025.

item.title: Britney Spears Spoke ‘Nonsensically’ During D.U.I. Arrest, Records Say
item.description: In highway patrol videos of her arrest, Ms. Spears talks to officers in a childlike voice and offers to make them lasagna.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens and Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: Valerie Macon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: California Highway Patrol found Adderall and an empty wine glass in Britney Spears’s vehicle when they pulled her over in March.

item.title: A New Film Makes My Hearing Condition a Superpower. It’s Not.
item.description: In “Tuner,” a former musician turned safecracker has painful hyperacusis. For him, it’s a hidden talent. Here’s what it’s really like.
item.dc:creator: Joyce Cohen

item.title: The Brothers Who Made Virginia Woolf the Talk of Cannes
item.description: Arie and Chuko Esiri take a team approach to their filmmaking, and the results with their Nigerian-set adaptation “Clarissa” have wowed the festival.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis
item.media:credit: Julie Sebadelha/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Chuko, left, and Arie Esiri at Cannes this week. Present-day Nigeria and 1920s England “are eerily similar,” Chuko said of their decision to adapt Virginia Woolf’s “Mrs. Dalloway.”

item.title: At DanceAfrica, When the Rules Disappear, the Party Begins
item.description: Brooklyn students are learning a traditional Ugandan dance for BAM’s festival this weekend. “You cannot shake your hips if you are stressed,” the Ugandan troupe leader told them.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert and Hiroko Masuike

item.title: An Artist Ponders Pond Scum, Humans and the Meaning of Life
item.description: Anicka Yi questions humans’ place in the world through work that focuses on some of the tiniest life-forms out there. At Storm King, muck-filled columns are her lab.
item.dc:creator: Travis Diehl and Elinor Kry
item.media:credit: Anicka Yi/Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York
item.media:description: Anicka Yi leans against one of the columns holding microbial life that make up her new exhibit at Storm King.

item.title: ‘The Mandalorian and Grogu’ Review: Baby Yoda Takes the Silver Screen
item.description: This franchise offshoot follows a hero whose face is obscured for most of the movie. Is “Star Wars” fighting with one arm tied behind its back?
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman
item.category: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu (Movie)

item.title: Elim Chan Becomes First Female Music Director of San Francisco Symphony
item.description: Elim Chan has been named the symphony’s next music director. She joins a small field of female conductors at American orchestras, as San Francisco rebuilds after Esa-Pekka Salonen.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:credit: Lanna Apisukh for The New York Times
item.media:description: “When it happened, I was like, ‘Oh my God — it’s time,’” the conductor Elim Chan said of her appointment in San Francisco. “It is groundbreaking. It’s another ceiling broken.”

item.title: ‘The Boroughs’ Review: ‘Stranger Things,’ the Senior Edition
item.description: In a new Netflix series from the Duffer brothers’ production company, the people battling the monsters don’t move as fast as they used to.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale

item.title: James McNeill Whistler at Tate Britain: The Fleeting Nature of Light, and of Life
item.description: A retrospective at Tate Britain brings together the American-born painter’s gauzy paintings and some of the provocative ideas that drove him.
item.dc:creator: Emily LaBarge
item.media:credit: Tate
item.media:description: “Nocturne: Blue and Gold — Old Battersea Bridge” (1872-5) by James McNeill Whistler.

item.title: ‘I Love Boosters’ Review: A Fashion-Forward Sense of Justice
item.description: Keke Palmer plays a shoplifter who sells high-end clothing at a deep discount in Boots Riley’s polychromatic new film.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips

item.title: Finding Our Shared Geography in Monologues
item.description: Kathryn Grody’s “The Unexpected 3rd,” Leslie Ayvazian’s “Mention My Beauty” and Liza Jessie Peterson’s “The Peculiar Patriot” are among the solo shows at the In the Bricks Festival.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes

item.title: Stephen Colbert’s Last ‘Late Show’ Features Famous Friends and Paul McCartney
item.description: Colbert was joined by famous friends, fellow hosts and special guests like Paul McCartney for the finale of “The Late Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: ‘Ladies First’ Review: A Nightmare for Men
item.description: Sacha Baron Cohen and Rosamund Pike star in this role-reversal workplace comedy where women rule the world.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: Women’s Museum Bill Sinks Amid Dispute Over Trump and Trans Issues
item.description: The legislation to build a Smithsonian museum to honor women became contentious after it was amended to say the museum could only recognize “biological” females.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: Rod Lamkey/Associated Press
item.media:description: Efforts to advance a Smithsonian museum to celebrate the achievements of American women suffered a setback when a bill to pinpoint a location for it on the National Mall failed to pass in the House.

item.title: Stream These 9 Movies and Shows Before They Leave Netflix in June
item.description: Among the highlights leaving for U.S. subscribers are the original “Sex and the City” and the only onscreen pairing of Harrison Ford and Liam Neeson.
item.dc:creator: Jason Bailey

item.title: Valie Export, Who Made Art With Her Naked Body, Dies at 85
item.description: In provocative performance pieces and films, she forced viewers to confront misogyny in the media and society at large.
item.dc:creator: Will Heinrich
item.media:credit: Joe Klamar/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Ms. Export at the opening of a retrospective of her work at the Albertina in Vienna in 2023. What distinguished her art, beyond its aggression and sheer volume, was its lucidity.

item.title: ‘Peanuts’ Music Owner Sues Interior Department and 3 Companies for Copyright Infringement
item.description: The owner of the “Peanuts” catalog would really like it if companies and the U.S. government stopped using its music without permission.
item.dc:creator: Aimee Ortiz
item.media:credit: ABC, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A scene from “A Charlie Brown Christmas.”

item.title: Michael Pennington, Commanding Shakespeare Actor, Dies at 82
item.description: A mainstay of the Royal Shakespeare Company, he later started a rival theater group. He also appeared in “Return of the Jedi.”
item.dc:creator: Fred Monyak
item.media:credit: Ruby Washington/The New York Times
item.media:description: Michael Pennington as King Lear with Lilly Englert as Cordelia at Theater for a New Audience in Brooklyn in 2014. “Mr. Pennington punctuates the expected blasts of rage with a quieter, introspective insight that is even more devastating,” a review in The New York Times said.

item.title: Submit Your Summer Reading Bucket List
item.description: Tell us what you read this summer, and enter the drawing for this year’s prize.

item.title: Mary Lovelace O’Neal, Whose Paintings Were Saturated in Black, Dies at 84
item.description: An artist and activist, she gained prominence with monumental canvases that she soaked in the darkest substance she could find.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:credit: Aubrey Trinnaman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mary Lovelace O’Neal at her studio in Oakland, Calif., in 2020.

item.title: ‘Indian Princesses’ Review: A Search for a True Identity
item.description: The five girls in Eliana Theologides Rodriguez’s new play, about a “Native-inspired” program that trafficked in stereotypes, find ways to create their own experiences.
item.dc:creator: Brittani Samuel

item.title: How Jeff Probst Spoiled Part of the ‘Survivor’ Season 50 Finale
item.description: Probst provided some live television magic on Wednesday by accidentally revealing the results of a segment that hadn’t aired.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Dominik Bindl/Getty Images
item.media:description: Jeff Probst at the “Survivor 50” premiere in New York in February.

item.title: ‘Passenger’ Review: On the Beaten Path
item.description: It’s my way on the highway: That’s the message a demon has for a van life couple in Andre Ovredal’s tense supernatural thriller.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg

item.title: ‘Tuner’ Review: Harmonies, Heists and Hurt
item.description: Leo Woodall shines in this somewhat odd but vibrant movie about a piano tuner with a rare and excruciating condition who begins to break bad.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: ‘Saccharine’ Review: How Far Would You Go to Lose Weight?
item.description: In this supernatural horror movie, a miracle drug made of human ashes upends a medical student’s life.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: ‘Manas’ Review: A Bleak Coming of Age
item.description: A 13-year-old girl living on the Brazilian island of Marajó finds herself trapped in cycles of abuse in this upsetting film.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: ‘Everybody to Kenmure Street’ Review: When the Neighborhood Won
item.description: A must-see documentary about a protest against a 2021 immigration raid in Glasgow shows the power of community.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: How Stephen Colbert, and Late Night, Evolved
item.description: James Poniewozik, our chief television critic, traces the way Stephen Colbert presided over a heyday of political comedy on late night.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik, Edward Vega and Laura Salaberry
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: Publishing’s Latest Piracy Problem: Audiobooks on YouTube
item.description: Illegal, synthetically narrated copies of “The Hunger Games,” hit self-help books and everything in between are increasingly common on the platform.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Gothic Fantasy Novels Filled With Dark Magic
item.description: The best-selling author Rachel Gillig recommends books that bring the haunted hallmarks of Gothic fiction into enchanted settings.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Gillig

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Pays His Respects to ‘The Late Show with Stephen Colbert’
item.description: Kimmel said he hoped others will also tune in to CBS “for the last time” to watch Thursday’s “Late Show” finale.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: The A.I. Threat to Audiobooks
item.description: Artificial intelligence has made pirated audiobooks faster to make and harder to detect. Our reporter Alexandra Alter tells us about the latest threat to the publishing industry.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter, Léo Hamelin and Laura Salaberry
item.category: Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone (Book)

item.title: Was a Story That Just Won a Literary Prize A.I.-Generated?
item.description: A respected literary magazine has published an award-winning short story many readers believe to be generated by artificial intelligence. Experts aren’t all so sure.
item.dc:creator: Valeriya Safronova
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times

item.title: Michaela Yearwood-Dan’s Art Blends Religious Themes With Lines From Her Diary
item.description: In a major new solo show, Michaela Yearwood-Dan’s kaleidoscopic paintings blend religious references with lines from her own diary.
item.dc:creator: Precious Adesina

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Delight to Your Day
item.description: Travel cake, telegrams and a possibly addictive word game.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Review: Feels Like Old Times With the Return of Tap City
item.description: Saved from extinction, the New York City Tap Festival is back with its charms and flaws intact.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert and Rachel Papo
item.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times

item.title: After Eurovision Final, Dismay That Politics Didn’t Play a Bigger Role
item.description: Politics isn’t supposed to influence the international song contest, but some people wanted relationships between countries to hold more sway in this year’s voting.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Martin Meissner/Associated Press, via Ap Photo/Martin Meissner
item.media:description: Alexandra Capitanescu representing Romania in the Eurovision Song Contest last week.

item.title: A Beloved Theater Festival Is Back. Let the Adventures Begin.
item.description: With the new play “Titans,” Clubbed Thumb’s Summerworks series opens with a sharp ensemble work.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: John Cage’s ‘Apartment House 1776’ Returns in Detroit
item.description: “Apartment House 1776,” written for the United States’ bicentennial, is getting an immersive new production with a skeptical twist.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Edgar and Nick Hagen
item.media:description: The Detroit Opera production of “Apartment House 1776” opens this week at the Cranbrook Art Museum.

item.title: ‘Modern Love’: Escape Rooms as the Cure for Heartbreak
item.description: Faced with a sudden end to her marriage, Lauren Bans found relief in an unexpected place.
item.dc:creator: Anna Martin, Reva Goldberg, Davis Land, Sara Curtis, Elisa Gutierrez, Lynn Levy, Daniel Ramirez, Dan Powell, Pat McCusker and Elisheba Ittoop

item.title: ‘Benedetta,’ ‘Good Boy’ and More Streaming Gems
item.description: Lusty nuns, insecure writers and a brave pooch lead this month’s under-the-radar recommendations on your subscription streaming services.
item.dc:creator: Jason Bailey

item.title: With ‘1536,’ Ava Pickett Is ‘the Charli XCX of Young Playwrights’
item.description: Ava Pickett just opened her play “1536” in London. Next up: a TV adaptation, then a project with the filmmaker Baz Luhrmann.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Dickson
item.media:credit: Tom Jamieson for The New York Times
item.media:description: “1536,” which reached the stage last year, is Ava Pickett’s first full-length play.

item.title: ‘Tom Clancy’s Jack Ryan: Ghost War’ Review: He’s Back for More
item.description: John Krasinski makes his feature-film debut in the Ryan role, alongside Michael Kelly and Wendell Pierce.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny
item.category: Tom Clancy's Jack Ryan: Ghost War (Movie)

item.title: Older People Are Still Underrepresented on TV, but Shows Like ‘The Boroughs’ Are Helping
item.description: While older people are still underrepresented on TV, the characters that are there have become richer and more multidimensional.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski
item.category: A Man on the Inside (TV Program)
Only Murders in the Building (TV Program)

item.title: For the Obama Center, Mark Bradford Paints a Fierce and Luminous Chicago
item.description: “City of the Big Shoulders” reflects the city’s strength and complexity. It took the artist five years to complete, mapping migration patterns and structural racism.
item.dc:creator: Elly Fishman
item.category: Obama, Barack H, Presidential Library and Museum
item.media:credit: Evan Jenkins for The New York Times
item.media:description: The painter Mark Bradford stands at the edge of Lake Michigan on Chicago’s South Side. The artist drew inspiration from both the Great Lake and the city’s industrial roots for his new work commissioned by the Obama Foundation.

item.title: Late Night Could Set a Clock by Trump’s Iran Attack Delays
item.description: Jimmy Kimmel noted that the president backs down on his own deadlines to bomb Iran “every Tuesday. It’s like a new episode of ‘NCIS.’”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: ‘Beaches’ to Close on Broadway Months Earlier Than Planned
item.description: A musical version of the 1980s tear-jerker will close months earlier than planned after opening in April to negative reviews and soft sales.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Beaches,” which was rushed to Broadway with a modest cast, had been scheduled to run through Sept. 6.

item.title: International Booker Prize 2026: ‘Taiwan Travelogue’ is First Winner Originally Written in Mandarin
item.description: “Taiwan Travelogue” is the first novel originally written in Mandarin to win the major award for fiction translated into English.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall

item.title: Barry Blaustein Dies at 71; Eddie Murphy Was His Muse
item.description: He helped create Buckwheat, Mister Robinson and other characters Mr. Murphy played on “Saturday Night Live,” and was a writer of films like “Coming to America.”
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:credit: Matt Carr/Getty Images
item.media:description: Barry Blaustein in 2010. A comedy writer, he was best known for the characters and screenplays he helped write for Eddie Murphy as Mr. Murphy rose to become a superstar.

item.title: 6 New Songs You Should Hear Now
item.description: Listen to Kurt Vile’s poetic rock, Nia Archives’s electric earworm and a new tune from Mike D.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: Michelle Gustafson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kurt Vile

item.title: 3 Ways Operas Speak to the Moment, With Success and Failure
item.description: New works, “The Post Office” and “Constance: A Confession,” and a revival of Barber’s “Vanessa” show companies attempting to capture these nebulous times.
item.dc:creator: Gabrielle Ferrari

item.title: Julie Mehretu and John Jasperse Find Common Ground
item.description: Julie Mehretu and John Jasperse are collaborating at the Marian Goodman Gallery: “How do we bring something to each other’s work that feels productive?”
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert
item.media:credit: Lila Barth for The New York Times

item.title: Broadway’s ‘Giant,’ With John Lithgow as Roald Dahl, Turns a Profit in 10 Weeks
item.description: The fast success of this play, about the children’s author Roald Dahl, is a rarity on Broadway, where most shows lose money.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: John Lithgow as Roald Dahl in “Giant.”

item.title: The Best of ‘S.N.L.’ Season 51: Sensitive Strippers and Regretful Moms
item.description: This season “Saturday Night Live” found new ways to satirize the Trump administration and said goodbye to one of its most valuable cast members.
item.dc:creator: Dave Itzkoff
item.media:credit: Will Heath/NBC, via Getty Images
item.media:description: In a memorable “Saturday Night Live” sketch this season, a woman (Ashley Padilla, with Tommy Brennan and Jane Wickline) reconsidered her support for President Trump.

item.title: A 16th-Century Sketch of Anne Boleyn More Likely Depicted Her Mother, an A.I. Analysis Finds
item.description: Using facial-recognition technology, scholars have concluded that a 500-year-old drawing labeled “Anna Bollein Queen” more likely showed her mother, Elizabeth Howard.
item.dc:creator: Katherine Kornei
item.media:credit: Royal Collection Enterprises Limited 2025/Royal Collection Trust

item.title: How the ‘Summer House’ Betrayal Stacks Up Against ‘Scandoval’
item.description: Not since the “Scandoval” on “Vanderpump Rules,” between Tom Sandoval and Ariana Madix, has a reality TV conflict so gripped the discourse. But the shock of “Scamanda” cuts deeper.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: Jocelyn Prescod/Bravo, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The announcement that Amanda Batula and West Wilson, co-stars of Bravo’s “Summer House,” were dating shocked many.

item.title: In Tides of Tomorrow, Desperate Times Call for Antediluvian Measures
item.description: In the eco-adventure Tides of Tomorrow, the actions of previous players shape the world you will encounter.
item.dc:creator: Christopher Byrd

item.title: Jon Stewart Asks What ‘Daddy’ Brought Back From China
item.description: “He was gone for a couple of days in China. We were scared,” Stewart said of President Trump on Monday’s “The Daily Show.” “But now he’s home, and I’m sure he brought us all the goodies.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
